sentences.text: And saying that I was to be taken to the Jewish cemetery.
They were to shoot me there, and forget the incidents, once and for all.
No.
He was the one--
The original one who was drunk--
--who was instrumental in bringing me to this camp.
When it was told to him that I had gone out with Schlesinger, and they were bitter enemies, he was afraid that I had to watch their secrets.
I didn't know their secrets.
I had an idea of what was going on there, but I couldn't put it together.
And even if I knew, would I have told them?
Of course not.
But I was under suspicion now, that I had undermined the structure of the camp and the payoff, and how it all hung together.
That was in 1943.
I was born in-- 20? 19?
Yeah.
But the man from the Judenrat, Manek was his name, said to this Untersturmfuhrer, I have a better idea.
Why should we sully our own hands?
Let me drive her to Budzyn.
That was a camp nearby.
And they had an Untersturmfuhrer called Feix.
He was the most sadistic man I had met up to then.
Feix will know what to do with her and we don't have to sully our hands.
So the Untersturmfuhrer agreed and we went on a truck with two SS men and this Jewish policeman.
And we drove to Budzyn, camp Budzyn.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And as I was shoved to the gate, I saw a man in Polish uniform with a three-cornered hat, who wore something on him that identified him as a Jew as well, prisoner of war and as a Jew.
Because I didn't know it at the time, but we looked at each other for just a moment.
And he had such beautiful, beautiful eyes.
And then the SS woman took me away and I had to go to the shower and have, you know, clothes that were too big.
And I looked totally different when I came out.
And I was ashamed to look at him.
But he winked at me.
This man later became my husband, after the war.
Camp Budzyn where Feix reigned was a lot harder.
And it was much more difficult.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked for an SS man there.
I had to scrub floors for time.
And his wife was relentless.
You know, I worked very hard, but I still had enough to eat.
Yeah.
Cleaning their house, I worked.
And I also had other jobs there.
And one day, I saw this Polish prisoner of war again.
He was still in his uniform.
And he said, ah, you're the little one that came that night.
That's so unusual because everybody comes in transports or a group of people.
You were all alone.
I didn't want to tell him what it was all about.
But he said to me, I work in the kitchen and there's a chef behind the kitchen.
Why don't you come tonight?
I'll bring you something to eat.
And we did.
And we met there almost nightly, and he brought me coffee, and bread, and underwear.
And we had really fallen in love.
And I told him the story and how bad I felt about it.
And he said anybody else would've done the same.
And I was still looking for my brother.
And the main street in the camp, we called it the [GERMAN].
It was like the Champs-Elysees for us, you know?
It's where we met people, where we traded, bartered, heard what happened.
And one day when I didn't even think about it, I felt someone tapping me on the shoulder.
It was-- looked like an old man, but it was a young boy.
It was my brother, my brother Sully.
He'd been there the whole time and I didn't know it.
He was so emaciated and so hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will never, never forget.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] A young boy.
I was able to get him to the hospital, because the doctor who had that hospital in Belzec was also the camp doctor here in Budzyn.
And he saw to it that he was kept there for a little while.
And I was able to give him my food because my friend Hillman gave me extra food.
But then eventually he had to go back to the barracks.
And he died in this camp.
He died of malnutrition, a collapsed lung, and beatings, and hunger.
And he was buried in a mass grave.
And I was alone.
[PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS]
I don't know if it was seven months or eight months.
But in Budzyn was an Untersturmfuhrer called Josef Leipold, the same Josef Leipold who was the Untersturmfuhrer at Schindler's camp in Brunnlitz.
Also in Budzyn were 50 Polish prisoners of war.
Hillman, Bernard Hillman who was later to become my husband, was one of the prisoners.
For some reason, this Untersturmfuhrer Leipold had some respect for these Polish prisoners of war.
He had them in positions where life was a little easier.
Like Bernard Hillman worked in the kitchen with a lot of them.
Some of them were heads of barracks, where you got a little extra food and had a little cubby hole to sleep in instead of on the planks.
And he also was friendly with this Dr.
[INAUDIBLE] a little bit.
And also was some German Jews.
There were few of us.
From Budzyn one day, we were all taken away on a train to Plaszow.
But before we got to Plaszow, we stopped at Poland's coal mines.
Not coal mines.
At salt mines in Wieliczka.
A few of the train-- what do you call them?
The-- Cars?
Cars were unhooked there and stayed there.
And the rest went on.
Of course, at the time, I didn't know they were going to Plaszow, but that's where they went.
I was in the car that was stopped, stopped in Wieliczka.
They needed workers there for a while.
And as I walked to that little camp, it was like a little labor camp, looking for Bernard Hillman and not finding him, I returned to my barrack.
When the barrack elder, the block elder, said to me, oh, Josef Leipold asked for you.
You are to work for him and his mistress in the villa over there.
You're going to clean house for him.
I was not thrilled because I knew what he was like, but I had no choice.
He wanted a German-speaking maid.
The next morning I reported to work.
And as I approached the villa, I could see that mezuzahs had hung there formally.
You know, I know from my own home, we had taken the mezuzah off before we left.
And even going up the stairs-- he lived on the third floor-- there were bottles embedded in the wallpaper that had been ripped off.
And to me, it indicated that Jewish people had lived there, hidden their valuables in bottles and wallpapered over it.
But it was all ripped up.
I came to the door and he told me, you know, what I had to do.
And his mistress, Lisa [INAUDIBLE], ordered me to go to the cellar and bring up hot water for her bath.
The hot water didn't come up to the third floor, so I took the two pails and I went, not just once, you know, to fill a bathtub.
I was very small, tiny, thin.
And I did it.
And according to her, I didn't bring the bath water up fast enough.
The bath water wasn't hot enough.
And I didn't know how to iron, and she complained about that.
After a day or two, she told the Untersturmfuhrer that I was lazy and sleazy.
And so he greeted me at the door, and he said, you go to the cellar now and I want to see how fast you can bring up the bath water.
And when I arrived back upstairs, he took the two pails and scalded me with the hot water.
And he said, see that window there?
That's the window I'm going to throw you out tomorrow if you don't do better.
But he had a change of heart and he dismissed me that same evening.
And I did not have to go back.
My friend, Ava, who lives is Israel now, was his next maid.
She was tall and sturdy.
And she stayed and worked for them.
I worked in the salt mines after that.
And one day when our work was done--
Oh, it was the eeriest things.
I described it in detail in my manuscript.
We had to shovel the salt off the walls.
It looked like a mountain, but it was salt.
And put it into lorries that went on--
On--
Tracks.
Yeah.
And it was mindless work.
It was hard work.
And I was very sick.
I had-- was forming gallstones at the time.
It was hard work, but it was not Plaszow.
It was not Auschwitz-Birkenau.
We had a little more to eat.
And one day this work was over and we were sent to Plaszow.
I had already heard that Bernard Hillman was in Plaszow because our food came from there and he smuggled a little note out.
And so this time it was Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what we had to do at Plaszow was smooth the earth over where the corpses had been dug out of.
At that time, the shootings and rampage that went down in Plaszow, as you've seen in was rampant.
And the bodies were just thrown into the pit.
The fire was burning.
A big fire was burning at one side.
At the other side, the bodies came out.
And you could see prisoners go to the mouth and take out the teeth and separate the gold fillings and the gold teeth, and hand them to couples, who we usually Ukrainian guards.
And they collected all that, searched the bodies.
The stench was so unbearable.
And the mere thought that you were handling human beings who were your brethren, who could be your relatives.
That was the greatest fear-- to see someone.
This is where Bernard Hillman and his detail and thousands of others worked.
We women had then to go and smooth out the earth around it and be witness to it.
The smell was so horrendous and the idea of it, it demoralized all of us.
I had not seen Bernard Hillman then.
But one day, when I came to work and a detail was marching back to camp, and I thought there was someone in there that resembled him.
But he didn't have his uniform on.
He was so emaciated.
It was him though.
Because he saw me and he lagged behind one day.
And he said, this is the end.
And when he first met me, he had said to me, what do you remember most about your home?
And I had told him about my father singing arias from operas, but vividly the lilac tree.
The lilac tree that reached up to my parents' bedroom.
And on my mother's birthday, on May 8th, my father would stand-- it was very romantic-- under the tree and sing, [GERMAN].
Think you can translate that?
And he said to me, if we live, I want to marry you and I will a plant you a lilac tree.
[CRYING]
In Plaszow he said to me, I don't think I'll ever plant that lilac tree now because I can't live.
I can't go on.
This is it.
I cannot go on.
I wrote him a little note and had it smuggled to him that he may feel he could release himself from that promise, but I hadn't released him yet.
No way could he do that to me.
A promise is a promise.
And so I see throughout as I wrote this down, I realized how we supported one another.
Not with food alone-- with thought, with strength, to hold out and hold out.
Not that that would have prevented the Nazis from doing away with us.
But it was also chancy.
But if you had the support of someone, it certainly aided you in wanting to go on.
I myself had entertained the thought of throwing myself on the barbed wire after my brother died.
I felt-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't live with that.
But also then he counseled me.
So we were in Plaszow.
And one day he came to the fence and he said to me, I just found out something.
There is a list here, a list about a man named Oskar Schindler.
And guess who's going to be Untersturmfuhrer in the camp that he wants to establish?
I had no idea what he was talking about.
And he said, Leipold, Josef Leipold.
And he's put the Polish prisoners of war on the list.
That means I'm going.
And guess what?
Your name is on the list.
And so I sadly told him, that couldn't be because he had threatened to throw me out a window.
He scalded me with hot water.
No way.
He said, yup.
Your name is on it.
Nobody can change it if your name is on it.
I was astounded.
In the meantime, I had contacted scarlet fever.
I was in the hospital.
Again, my good friend Dr.
[INAUDIBLE] took care of me.
And I didn't know what happened to the list, or the women, or the men who were to go to Schindler's list.
And when I was released from that hospital again, under the guidance of Dr.
[INAUDIBLE], I saw the women of Krakow who I knew by then were on that list.
They were still there.
However, the men had left already.
They had gone what we believed to be Brunnlitz, the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shortly after that, there were selections and selections.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a miracle that I survived them, because I was very, very thin looking and pale.
And I did survive it and I went on to Auschwitz with the 300 women that Schindler had asked for.
And we took a little detour to Auschwitz.
I was in the dead line.
I got myself out of the dead line by walking away.
To Auschwitz?
It was night.
It was cold.
Perhaps it was snowing.
And there was a lot of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] noise, dogs balking.
The first time that I saw SS women in the long, black capes, and they had riding whips.
And we saw a man in striped uniform putting up these ramps so we could climb out of the cars-- out of the cattle cars.
Schnell Schnell, you know.
They got us out of there.
And we were lined up.
And it wasn't only us.
There were many others there too.
It must have been different transports.
And we were quickly separated and had to go to a line where, I believe it was Mengele who was sitting there with other doctors.
And some of us said, [GERMAN].
We have to go to Brunnlitz.
But the minute we opened our mouth, you know, the riding whip-- shut up.
What is the equivalent in German?
[GERMAN].
They used very foul language.
And it took forever to get to the gate that said [GERMAN].
And we ended up in Birkenau in a little mud hut, not far from the crematorium.
And by then of course, we knew.
And our hut was always full, gray with with ashes.
And sometimes children were marched by.
And I wrote a poem that was published in '84.
It's called 'The Children of Auschwitz.' I shall never forget the children of Auschwitz.
My own misery was paled against the children of Auschwitz.
It is something that one can never, never forget, how those innocent children were coaxed with sweet words by SS men.
[GERMAN] Your mother is waiting for you.
Come on.
And marching them straight to the ovens, straight to the gas chambers and the ovens.
It is something too horrendous to even think about.
I don't let myself think about it very often.
But when it comes I have to run away from home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's unbearable.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I walk by the sea and I talk to myself.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I think you know the rest already, that we were then from Auschwitz, three weeks later, sent to Brunnlitz.
I had never seen Oskar Schindler before.
But there he was.
Well, the train ride from Auschwitz to Brunnlitz was horrendous.
We had a pail to use as a toilet.
It was overflowing.
And when we stopped somewhere, an SS man pointed to it and said-- pointed to the women and said, you there.
Pick up that pail.
He pointed to someone who had lots of brothers in Plaszow, and who was sort of a little privileged yet.
And she didn't move.
No one moved.
And I knew that he could shoot us.
So I took that pail and edged it-- thin as I was, maybe 70 pounds-- walked down with it.
And got it all over me.
And he cursed me and cursed me.
And I emptied the pail.
I got back on the train.
And one of the other women noticed what had happened.
And she said, he didn't point at you.
Why did you take it?
And I explained it to her.
And she got me a blanket, a ragged blanket, and she stood watch over me.
And in that condition, I arrived in Brunnlitz.
I was also very ill.
I had a head cold.
I'd had meningitis as a young girl, so I always suffered from head colds.
And we got off the train.
And there were SS men there.
But there was Oskar Schindler.
And someone pointed him out to me and said, see that man?
That's him.
And he was so kind and so friendly.
And we got a shower and we got food.
And the man stood on the balcony of their building, and there was a voice that said, little one, little one.
Here.
Look up.
It's me.
That was Bernard Hillman.
But I couldn't trust it.
I thought it's an illusion.
It couldn't be him.
I couldn't make myself believe it was him.
Only much later in the afternoon, after I'd slept a little, I realized-- yeah.
And we met that night, by a mesh wire that separated the women's balcony from the men's balcony.
And he had bought hot coffee, as he had done in Budzyn, and bread and socks.
And he said, as soon as quarantine is over for you, I have a position for you in the SS kitchen.
Don't worry about it.
They're not going to hurt you.
And you'll have more food there.
And so it was.
I worked there.
I scrubbed the floors and the sinks.
And I did peeling potatoes and vegetables, and helping get the breakfast ready.
And one day, I met an SS man there, whom we called Crazy Johan.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
He beat up prisoners.
He was awful, awful.
And he had a fight that morning.
And he always complained about the breakfasts.
And he came to the kitchen door and he threw the eggs and everything at me, and he said, [GERMAN].
The eggs were too hard.
And he said that in an [GERMAN] dialect.
And I never heard him speak in dialect.
So I answered him in dialect, saying, I'm so sorry.
I'll make you good eggs.
And his mouth stood open.
He said, you speak [GERMAN].
Where are you from?
And I said, Aurich.
Well, he was from a little town nearby called [INAUDIBLE] where very poor people lived in mud huts.
And he said, your name is Wolff?
Well, I know Wolff this and Wolff that.
It was my grandfather he knew.
And I was afraid to say that I was associated with all these people.
I didn't know what dealings he had had with them.
So I said, I've been away from there so long, I don't really remember it all.
From that day on, he stopped hitting the prisoners, and he never, ever did anything bad to me.
And things were better in that kitchen.
Why do you think that was?
He had never dealt with anyone from his home territory, you know?
And suddenly he sees someone.
He was of a very low class, suddenly had gotten the power and the position to lord it over others.
And then he's transformed back into his beginnings.
And he sees this one German Jewish woman who knows all about his background, who knows where he came from.
Oh it was terrifying.
Terrifying.
They would say, did you wash your hands?
How many lice did you count this morning in your hair?
Always degrading and demeaning.
And I only answered when I was spoken to.
Never did I say anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
It was terrifying.
But Oskar Schindler would come to the kitchen door from time to time and say, well, how's it going?
They treat you all right?
Or Frau Schindler neglected her wifely duties this morning.
You think you can make breakfast for me?
And when Leipold came to the door, I shrank.
I ran into a corner.
I didn't want him to recognize me.
No.
He might have, but he didn't let on.
So it's so ironic that I came to be alive today because of this German man, this Untersturmfuhrer.
It's so ironic.
He threatened to kill me, and yet because he insisted his Jews get on that list, I'm alive today.
He said he wanted the 50 Polish prisoners of war, and he wanted two German Jewish girls-- my friend Ava, who lives in Ramat Gan, and myself.
I'm a religious person and I believe that it was HaShem's will.
That HaShem guided him somehow, that I lived to-- I don't know-- perhaps produce a son.
[CRYING] I don't know.
Don't know.
